coredata.dc:title: Analysis of the efficiency, performance, and operational capacity of a high-voltage battery in an autonomous photovoltaic system for water supply

Orts-Grau, Salvador
Gasque, María
González-Altozano, Pablo
Gimeno-Sales, Francisco-José
Segui-Chilet, Salvador
coredata.dc:description:
The integration of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) into photovoltaic (PV) systems—both grid-connected and stand-alone—is increasingly common. This study presents experimental results from a multipurpose PV water pumping system equipped with an 11.5 kWh, 230.4 V LIB. Independent tests were conducted to characterize the charging and discharging processes, as well as the efficiencies and performance of the system, under real operating conditions. LIB full charge was performed exclusively with PV energy. Two distinct phases were identified: a fast and efficient charge to state of charge (SOC) values of between 87% and 95%, with the PV array at the maximum power point (MPP); and a slow and less efficient phase reaching near full charge (SOC ≈ 100%). In this second phase, energy curtailment occurred due to battery management system limitations. These caused deviation from the MPP and resulted in wasted energy. During the transition between charging phases, a sudden rise in LIB voltage and a drop in PV power were observed. The LIB was discharged at constant power using the variable speed drive (VSD) of the water pumping system with maximum efficiency observed at lower frequencies in VSD operation. Converter efficiencies showed optimal values (>94%) at power levels above 500 W in the DC/DC conversion during charging from the PV field. During discharge, the greatest efficiency in the DC/AC conversion occurred at the lowest frequency (37 Hz). Operating the VSD at low power is recommended to maximize energy use, even if the water flow lessens. Battery capacity estimation using various SOC windows (W
SOC ) revealed a high variability at W
SOC = 1%, while the 40–80% SOC range yielded the most reliable results. Final charging stages should be avoided due to PV energy losses and capacity underestimation. Performance ratios confirmed significant energy losses at the end of charging, which suggests the need to increase AC consumption through diversion loads to maximize PV generation. These findings offer valuable insights for the design, modeling, and operation of PV systems with LIB storage.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper presents the experimental characterization and performance analysis of a high-voltage lithium-ion battery (LIB(HV)) integrated into a photovoltaic WPS (PVWPS). The study investigates the battery's charging behavior under real operating conditions, distinguishing between fast and limited-power (slow) charging stages, and examines the influence of LIB voltage and current, as well as solar irradiance, on the battery's SOC. The performance of the battery management system (BMS) is evaluated to assess its role in regulating and protecting the battery during operation [36] [37] . The discharge process is also characterized using a variable speed drive (VSD) of a WPS operating at different frequencies to identify key parameters that affect energy delivery and the relationship between SOC and LIB voltage. Additionally, the study analyzes the LIB current-voltage dynamics during fast charging and discharging phases, providing insights into process efficiency. Finally, the battery's usable capacity and round-trip performance ratio are estimated to evaluate the overall effectiveness of the EES within the PV application. This research is developed in the pilot pumping facility utilizing high-voltage LIBs as presented in [38] , within the scope of the project \u201CGreening Humanitarian Responses through Enhanced Solar Energy Harvesting\u201D. This project was developed as an application within the smart water management category, as described in [14] . Funding was provided by the International Organization for Migration, the USAID's Bureau for Humanitarian Assistance, and the European Commission Humanitarian Aid and Civil Protection.

This work was supported in part by the Universitat Polit\u00E8cnica de Val\u00E8ncia (UPV - Program ADSIDEO-cooperation 2017 - project \u2018Characterization of sustainable systems for the pumping of water for human consumption in developing regions and/or refugee camps in Kenya through the implementation of isolated photovoltaic systems with new generation lithium-ion batteries\u2019), in part by the International Organization for Migration under a contract entitled \u2018Greening humanitarian responses through enhanced Solar Energy harvesting\u2019 with funding from the Bureau for Humanitarian Assistance-USAID and Humanitarian Aid department of the European Commission (ECHO), and in part by grant TED2021-131137B-100 funded by MCIN/AEI /10.13039/501100011033 and by the European Union NextGenerationEU/PRTR (project 'Contribution to the ecological transition in the industrial sector through photovoltaic self-consumption').

To Javier P\u00E9rez, Xavier Busquets, and Monica Zheng from SolaX Power Co ( https://www.solaxpower.com/ ), and Antonio Ramos (Technosun - https://b2b.technosun.com/ ) for the donation of a hybrid inverter with the corresponding high-voltage lithium battery. To Ignacio Valdeolmillos (Carlo Gavazzi Group ( https://automation.gavazzi.es/home - monitoring and data acquisition system) for his involvement in the project. To Sergio Ure\u00F1a (Fuji Electric Europe - https://www.fujielectric-europe.com/ ) for his support for the project. To the Vice-Chancellor of Infrastructure at the UPV for the financial support that has enabled the project's electrical installation to be completed.

This work was supported in part by the Universitat Polit\u00E8cnica de Val\u00E8ncia (UPV - Program ADSIDEO-cooperation 2017 - project \u2018Characterization of sustainable systems for the pumping of water for human consumption in developing regions and/or refugee camps in Kenya through the implementation of isolated photovoltaic systems with new generation lithium-ion batteries\u2019), in part by the International Organization for Migration under a contract entitled \u2018Greening humanitarian responses through enhanced Solar Energy harvesting\u2019 with funding from the Bureau for Humanitarian Assistance-USAID and Humanitarian Aid department of the European Commission (ECHO), and in part by grant TED2021-131137B-100\u00A0funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033 and by the European Union NextGenerationEU/PRTR (project 'Contribution to the ecological transition in the industrial sector through photovoltaic self-consumption'). To Javier P\u00E9rez, Xavier Busquets, and Monica Zheng from SolaX Power Co (https://www.solaxpower.com/), and Antonio Ramos (Technosun -https://b2b.technosun.com/) for the donation of a hybrid inverter with the corresponding high-voltage lithium battery. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The integration of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) into photovoltaic (PV) systems—both grid-connected and stand-alone—is increasingly common. This study presents experimental results from a multipurpose PV water pumping system equipped with an 11.5 kWh, 230.4 V LIB. Independent tests were conducted to characterize the charging and discharging processes, as well as the efficiencies and performance of the system, under real operating conditions.

LIB full charge was performed exclusively with PV energy. Two distinct phases were identified: a fast and efficient charge to state of charge (SOC) values of between 87% and 95%, with the PV array at the maximum power point (MPP); and a slow and less efficient phase reaching near full charge (SOC ≈ 100%). In this second phase, energy curtailment occurred due to battery management system limitations. These caused deviation from the MPP and resulted in wasted energy. During the transition between charging phases, a sudden rise in LIB voltage and a drop in PV power were observed. The LIB was discharged at constant power using the variable speed drive (VSD) of the water pumping system with maximum efficiency observed at lower frequencies in VSD operation.

Converter efficiencies showed optimal values (>94%) at power levels above 500 W in the DC/DC conversion during charging from the PV field. During discharge, the greatest efficiency in the DC/AC conversion occurred at the lowest frequency (37 Hz). Operating the VSD at low power is recommended to maximize energy use, even if the water flow lessens.

Battery capacity estimation using various SOC windows (W
SOC ) revealed a high variability at W
SOC = 1%, while the 40–80% SOC range yielded the most reliable results. Final charging stages should be avoided due to PV energy losses and capacity underestimation. Performance ratios confirmed significant energy losses at the end of charging, which suggests the need to increase AC consumption through diversion loads to maximize PV generation. These findings offer valuable insights for the design, modeling, and operation of PV systems with LIB storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of solar energy, specifically its application to water pumping, stands out as a sustainable solution in developing rural areas with high irradiation levels [1–2]. Photovoltaic (PV) pumping installations therefore constitute an important building block in the economic progress of these communities [3], where access to water guarantees compliance with several of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) set by the United Nations [4], including SDG 7 (access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all), SDG 6 (clean water & sanitation), and SDG 13 (climate action) [5].

The main objective in many research works is to maximize energy generation and the efficiency of water pumping systems (WPS) [6–7]. To improve the operation of the installations and provide greater versatility (especially for water for domestic use), an energy storage system (ESS) can be included [8]. Among the existing options, the most common is the use of a water tank [9–10]. A water tank has traditionally been the solution to avoid the increased cost of incorporating batteries as an energy storage system. This requires the addition of an adequate control and monitoring system, along with additional maintenance, repair, and replacement costs.

However, using batteries to pump water in these facilities, either alone or in combination with a water storage tank, offers significant advantages for populations in isolated rural areas. This option enables pumping if water consumption does not match the solar resource and ensures a continuous supply and WPS autonomy during low solar radiation or due to variations in irradiance caused by passing clouds, as is proposed in [11]. The use of an electric car as a BESS for pumping and mitigating fluctuations in the operating point due to fluctuations in irradiance is analyzed in [12]. The study simulates three scenarios with different operating points for the PV system and the BESS, performing a dynamic analysis of the system using a solar simulator. The inclusion of a BESS also makes electricity available for other uses such as lighting, preservation of medicines, and charging mobile phones [13–14]. Common battery technologies in PV ESS systems are lithium-ion and lead-acid [15–16].

Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gained popularity in renewable and sustainable energy applications, especially in photovoltaics [17–18]. LIBs offer significant advantages compared to lead-acid batteries, such as higher energy and power density, longer life, lower weight and size, wider operating temperature range, less maintenance, and faster charging capability [19–20], although their price must fall for these batteries to become the most common solution in off-grid ESS. The reviews published in [21–22] included a brief summary of the standard technologies used in battery energy storage systems (BESSs), and their most notable characteristics. Studies such as [23] emphasize the importance of utilizing realistic LIB models to obtain accurate estimates, highlighting that the battery state of charge (SOC) level is crucial in determining the remaining energy available in a battery.

The growing need for advanced rechargeable LIBs for PV systems (as well as portable electronics, smart grids, and electric vehicles) has given rise to considerable research in recent decades. In the review published in [24], the latest developments in high-energy density LIBs are outlined, and the current industry challenges delaying advances in this technology are addressed. An overview of storage systems for residential applications is offered in [25], together with battery technologies and BESS power electronic converters. The authors conclude that, although most residential BESSs currently use low-voltage batteries, converter schemes for high-voltage solutions will become more popular in the near future.

A battery's lifespan depends on several factors: materials and manufacturing quality; electrochemistry; environmental location; number of charges and discharges; and charging and discharging [26]. As the optimization of the discharge process depends on energy use and connected devices, a detailed understanding of degradation mechanisms and an appropriate charging strategy are essential to improve the life and efficiency of batteries [27].

Several previous studies have evaluated charging and discharging processes, as well as the performance of LIB-based BESS, in search of optimal energy efficiency and system performance. The considerable volume of high-quality literature addressing this subject—one of the most widely discussed topics according to [28]—leads us to highlight only those works most closely related to the line of research developed here. A model is developed [29] based on an equivalent resistive circuit to characterize charge and discharge efficiencies and power limits for steady-state LIB operation, considering variability and dependence on the battery SOC and power demand.

The methodology established in [30] to determine the charge and discharge characteristics of a BESS using LIBs involved simultaneously recording power, SOC, and battery temperature when the storage system was in regular operation. A comparative analysis of two approaches to battery degradation models (named physical-chemical aging and semiempirical) presented in [31] concludes that the inclusion of a BESS in a PV installation has a significant impact on economic factors associated with the operational aspects, including energy capacity, overall efficiency, and degradation over the life cycle. It should be noted that system data, such as battery characteristics or converter efficiencies, were part of the input parameters for the semiempirical model. The authors also established that the main parameters influencing life cycle behavior were the discharging/charging rate (C-rate), the battery depth of discharge (DOD), and the operating temperature ([32], as cited in [31]). It is also worth mentioning the model suggested in [33] to demonstrate how the efficiency of BESSs with LIBs degrades over time. Their findings reveal that ambient temperature, discharge current, and cutoff voltage have distinct impacts on LIB performance. This model could prove valuable in enhancing the battery performance ratio by adjusting operational parameters or developing improved management strategies. In some studies, such as in [34], off-grid WPS models were developed to maximize the volume pumped during the year, resulting in a system with 3 MW of PV and 1.9 MW of BESS. The review published in [35] offers a detailed analysis of the causes that can lead to the degradation or aging of LIBs, and highlights the following: operating conditions (such as current, voltage, and temperature); strategies during charging and discharging; self-discharge current; overcharge; number of cycles; DOD; and battery storage temperatures. Finally, and in accordance with some of the previously cited studies, battery SOC and DOD, charge/discharge currents, and high temperatures, are also pointed out as causes for battery degradation in [21], with the battery's remaining useful life, and state of health (SOH), being decisive factors in predicting LIB degradation (with a special emphasis on high temperatures).

It is essential to note that most studies rely on models or algorithms to analyze various scenarios and identify factors affecting battery energy efficiency or degradation over a short period. This approach is cheaper and occupies less space than experimental studies. However, experimental results and verification of the real behavior of batteries are necessary to validate models and assess performance in real conditions. For this reason, studies that experimentally evaluate the charge/discharge processes of batteries in PV installations are important.

This paper presents the experimental characterization and performance analysis of a high-voltage lithium-ion battery (LIB(HV)) integrated into a photovoltaic WPS (PVWPS). The study investigates the battery's charging behavior under real operating conditions, distinguishing between fast and limited-power (slow) charging stages, and examines the influence of LIB voltage and current, as well as solar irradiance, on the battery's SOC. The performance of the battery management system (BMS) is evaluated to assess its role in regulating and protecting the battery during operation [36] [37]. The discharge process is also characterized using a variable speed drive (VSD) of a WPS operating at different frequencies to identify key parameters that affect energy delivery and the relationship between SOC and LIB voltage. Additionally, the study analyzes the LIB current-voltage dynamics during fast charging and discharging phases, providing insights into process efficiency. Finally, the battery's usable capacity and round-trip performance ratio are estimated to evaluate the overall effectiveness of the EES within the PV application. This research is developed in the pilot pumping facility utilizing high-voltage LIBs as presented in [38], within the scope of the project “Greening Humanitarian Responses through Enhanced Solar Energy Harvesting”. This project was developed as an application within the smart water management category, as described in [14]. Funding was provided by the International Organization for Migration, the USAID's Bureau for Humanitarian Assistance, and the European Commission Humanitarian Aid and Civil Protection.

The rest of the paper is structured as follows: Section 2 begins with a brief literature review on the topic, followed by a description of the experimental tests conducted, detailing the evolution of the main magnitudes recorded by the monitoring system used in the facility. The charging process (differentiated phases for fast and slow charging) is characterized, and the influence of LIB voltage and current, as well as solar irradiance, on the SOC is analyzed. The operation of the BMS is also assessed. The discharge process is examined to identify the main parameters affecting energy delivery and the SOC–voltage relationship. Finally, the section explores the current–voltage correlation during fast charging and discharging, providing a comparative analysis of process efficiency. Section 3 focuses on the analysis of the battery efficiency and the performance ratio obtained during the experimental tests. It begins by evaluating efficiency behavior during both charging and discharging tests. The usable capacity of the battery is then estimated by using several sliding SOC windows under the test operating conditions, followed by a detailed study of the unreliable results obtained when including the final stage of LIB charging. Finally, battery performance is evaluated during the fast and slow charging phases. The final conclusions summarize the key insights derived from the study, highlighting the most relevant aspects of the behavior during the charging and discharging processes of a LIB with a nominal capacity of 11.5 kWh.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental charging and discharging tests of a LIB in an off-grid PV system for water pumping
ce:section.ce:para:
The study and analysis of lithium batteries can be carried out considering several key factors, including energy density, energy storage capacity, charge/discharge ratios, efficiency, voltage stability, and current stability. The performance ratio of the battery (PR
LIB ) during tests, referred to as round-trip energy efficiency in [39], is calculated in the present study from (1), where E
LIB_DCH and E
LIB_CH represent the total energy discharged and charged in a test cycle, or within a given SOC interval. The internal resistance of the battery and the energy dissipated as heat during charging and discharging are the main factors affecting the values obtained for PR
LIB and are also important for determining aging effects in batteries [16]. PR
LIB values between 90.63% and 97.05% for a 20 Ah@3.2 V battery, and from 86.53% to 95.27% for a 25 Ah@3.2 V battery, were reported in [39] for different charging and discharging C-rates and temperatures. A charge/discharge efficiency equal to 93% was used in the PV grid-connected system that included a battery coupled to the AC bus through its own inverter [40], while in [41] the value used in MATLAB to simulate an 8 MW utility-scale PV system with 47 MWh of BESS was equal to 95%. Values of PR
LIB for different BESS were reported in [42].
(1)
PR LIB
=
E
LIB _ DCH
E
LIB _ CH

The starting point for this study is the prototype of a multipurpose PV water pumping system with a high voltage LIB (PVWPS+LIB(HV)) facility, characterized and analyzed in [38]. The installation is equipped with an X1-Hybrid-3.7-D-E grid-connected inverter from SOLAX, which has a rated apparent power of 3680 VA in the grid-connected output and 4 kVA in the emergency power supply (EPS), with a maximum of 6 kVA for 10 s in the EPS output [43]. The LIB(HV) is the model T-BAT SYS-HV (consisting of a T-BAT H 5.8 and one HV11550 battery pack) from SOLAX, with a total energy storage capacity of 11.5 kWh (equivalent to 50 Ah), a maximum DOD (10.35 kWh usable energy) of 90%, a maximum power of 3.5 kW, a maximum charge/discharge current of 35 A, a recommended charge/discharge current of 25 A, and a rated voltage of 230.4 V (elements connected in series) [44]. A brief review of the literature regarding different options for high-voltage battery-based water pumping systems was included in [38]. The remaining main components and configuration of this installation, as well as the monitoring system, were previously detailed in other preceding studies developed by the authors of this paper ([45–47]). The resolution of the different parameters available from the hybrid inverter, as specified in the manufacturer's communications manual [48], can be summarized as follows: voltage 0.1 V, current 0.1 A, power 1 W (1 VA and 1 VAr), frequency 0.01 Hz, battery state of charge 1%, and temperature 1 °C.

In this paper, a series of cyclic tests was performed to characterize the behavior of the LIB(HV) under the charging and discharging operating conditions of the PVWPS+LIB(HV) installation. For that purpose, a set of six days of the same week was selected, from May 2 to May 7, 2023 (DOY 122 to DOY 127). To carry out these tests, every day during this week, the battery was first charged from the early morning hours with the energy obtained from the PV field until it reached its full charge (SOC = 100%). In contrast, in other works, the full charge state in lead-acid batteries was SOC > 90% [49]. To ensure that the charge process did not interfere with the discharge process, the battery was subsequently discharged during the hours without solar irradiance, at a constant power set by the operating frequency of the VSD driving the pump, and during a single operating cycle, as is common in real installations, avoiding the pulsating operation used in [7]. The tests were conducted at an ambient temperature of (21.13 ± 4.22) °C with the battery temperature being (28.72 ± 1.36) °C (values of mean ± standard deviation), the latter ranging from a minimum of 26 °C and a maximum of 32 °C. Using the monitoring system presented in [46] the values of the main parameters of the installation were acquired every 1 min (recording interval t
k = 1 min) and analyzed in MATLAB.

Fig. 1 shows the evolution of the global irradiance (GI), PV power (P
PV ), LIB power (P
LIB ), SOC, LIB voltage (V
LIB ), and LIB current (I
LIB ) during charge and discharge tests. In a preliminary analysis of the plots, it can be appreciated that:
•
LIB charging begins with a rapid charging stage followed by a low-power or slow charging stage. Rapid charging stage is characterized by a gradual increase in V
LIB and I
LIB . I
LIB in turn, is conditioned by the evolution of GI., which is followed by a low-power or slow charging stage.
•
The discharging starts with a fast-discharging stage due to the VSD connection, and concludes with a system standby discharging stage (where I
LIB values are stable and close to zero) until the start of the new solar day.

The most significant parameters relating to test conditions are detailed in Table 1 for the charge tests with a variable input power (equal to the PV power) and for the discharge tests, set with a constant power consumption. The following variables are included: peak sun hours (PSH, in kWh/m2/day, equivalent to the irradiation); fast charge (F-CH) and fast discharge (F-DCH) time intervals (in hh:mm format); SOC range (expressed on a per unit basis); active power in the LIB (P
LIB , positive and variable during charging; and negative for the discharging process); frequency in the VSD output (f
VSD ); and the input power to the VSD connected to the AC output of the hybrid inverter (P
VSD_in ), measured with a Fluke 435-SII power quality analyzer as explained in [47].

In Fig. 1, fast charging intervals are highlighted with a blue rectangle in the SOC plot and correspond to positive values of P
LIB that produce a positive increase of the SOC (ΔSOC > 0), starting the process with SOC values between 22% and 39% depending on the final conditions of the slow discharge in the previous day. During charging intervals, P
PV and P
LIB are quite similar due to the low losses in the DC/DC converter that manages the maximum power point tracking (MPPT) of the PV generator and the DC/DC converter that manages the LIB operation. The fast-discharging intervals are highlighted with a pink rectangle in the SOC plot and are easily identified by the negative and constant values of P
LIB , with ΔSOC < 0. The f
VSD value for each day is included in the P
LIB plot to highlight the conditions of the discharge test, corresponding to the P
LIB value in Table 1. Discharge tests began when the GI was nil and with SOC values around 100% depending on the BMS that controls the LIB operation [35]. A clear gap is observed between the end of every fast charge test (which occurred before 16:00 on the day with the lowest PSH, the most unfavorable case) and the beginning of the next fast discharge test (which was always after 20:30). The charge and discharge periods were independent and did not overlap at any time during the tests except on DOY 122. A previous discharge of the LIB with a constant power of 2000 W (approx.) was performed at the beginning of this day to establish a specific initial SOC before starting the first full LIB charging process. The objective of this action was to standardize the charging and discharging processes as much as possible, reaching a similar SOC value at the end of every test day.

The values of the variables that define and delimit the charge and discharge periods are detailed in Table 2 , where V
LIB_start is the LIB voltage at the start of fast charge/discharge; V
LIB_end is the LIB voltage reached by the battery at the end of fast charge/discharge; P
PV_AV is the average PV power at charge/discharge, P
LIB_AV is the average LIB's charge/discharge power (discharge power depending on f
VSD ).

The LIB voltage at the start of the charge has an average value of V
LIB_start_CH_AV = 235.03 V, while at the end of the charge V
LIB_end_CH_AV = 246.90 V, which means a LIB voltage variation (ΔV
LIB_CH ) of +11.87 V, considerably lower than that which would be obtained with an equivalent lead-acid battery [50]. Regarding the discharge test, the average LIB voltage value at the start (V
LIB_start_DCH ) is 237.60 V, while at the end, V
LIB_end_DCH is equal to 231.33 V, with ΔV
LIB_DCH = −6.27 V.

In the following subsections, the main features of charge and discharge tests are analyzed. The LIB charge is first evaluated (both the slow and fast charge stages), considering the characteristic magnitudes during the process. Other aspects related to the charge stages are studied such as the interrelation of V
LIB , I
LIB , and GI, with SOC in order to assess whether the variability of the climatic conditions on the days of study (greater or lesser cloudiness/irradiance level) affect the charge level or its pattern, or the BMS behavior and its influence in the control of the PV point of operation established for fulfilling the LIB charge. Furthermore, LIB discharge is evaluated by analyzing the main parameters characterizing the fast and slow discharge stages, as well as the interrelation between SOC and V
LIB during this process. Finally, the relationship between I
LIB and V
LIB is established for both the fast-charging stage on sunny days and the fast-discharging stage. A fitting equation is inferred, enabling the determination of two characteristic parameters of the LIB (its electromotive/counter-electromotive force and internal resistance) and allowing for a comparative analysis of these parameters during charging and discharging processes.

Fig. 2 shows the evolution of the main variables in the system during the charge period on a partly cloudy day (DOY 126). This day is considered representative of the rest of the days in terms of both the trend and specific values of the parameters represented. Throughout the first stage of charging (or fast charging), both P
PV and P
LIB follow the pattern evolution of GI, and the PV field operates in the MPP, setting V
LIB_start_CH at P
LIB > 21 W (approximately). During this stage, which lasts from 7:14 to 15:17 (Fig. 2), I
LIB also follows the evolution of GI, and all the available PV energy is used to charge the LIB. Therefore, the fast charge period remains until the SOC is between 87% and 95% (maximum SOC value included in Table 1), being 94% on DOY 126, and with values for V
LIB_end_CH always greater than 245 V (Table 2), being 247.56 V on DOY 126. This stage is equivalent to the bulk stage in lead-acid batteries, where the battery is charged with the full current that the PV generator can produce under the existing environmental conditions and operating at the MPP [50]. Although the conditions that improve or worsen charge efficiency in lead-acid batteries were clearly established in [49], two different situations occur throughout the day in PV charging. Thus, low SOC and low C-rate (high efficiency) arise at the beginning of the day, but as noon approaches, both SOC and C-rate increase, leading to a reduction in charging efficiency. The fast charge phase concludes with a sudden rise in V
LIB followed by a sharp drop in both V
LIB and I
LIB .

The final charge stage that follows the fast charge concludes with SOC values of between 98% and 100% (99% on DOY 126) as shown in Table 1. The BMS, in addition to the functions described in [30], controls the power flow, reducing P
LIB and I
LIB to safe values that enable the full charge of the LIB to be completed. A sharp rise in V
LIB is observed at the end of this process, which occurs at approximately 15:17 h (Fig. 2), possibly due to the change in the LIB charge control strategy. As indicated in [51], appropriate control of upper voltage limits can help reduce the risk of unsafe conditions, improve battery efficiency and system compatibility, and extend battery operating life. This slow charge stage extends while P
PV > 0 W from V
LIB_end_CH to the beginning of the fast discharge (I
LIB < 0 A), a process that is established in these tests just after there is no possibility of PV generation (GI ≈ 0 W/m2). This stage is equivalent to the floating stage in lead-acid batteries. During this stage, the PV field is not operating at the MPP, and its output power (PV power) is intentionally reduced by the BMS.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Interrelation of V
LIB , I
LIB , and GI, with SOC
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 illustrates the interrelationship between V
LIB , I
LIB , and GI with SOC during the charging process on all study days. A V
LIB behavior similar to that described in [52] is observed despite the variability in charging conditions. On the sunniest days (DOYs 122 and 125), the evolution of both V
LIB and I
LIB showed a soft trend with fewer fluctuations than on the cloudiest days (124, 126, and especially 127). I
LIB fluctuations were more pronounced than V
LIB fluctuations on cloudy days, in line with GI fluctuations, and confirming that the dependence of I
LIB on GI is higher than that of V
LIB .

The graph of V
LIB clearly shows the transition from the fast charge stage (SOC values between 87% and 95%) to the slow charge stage (until the full charge, with SOC around 100%). As previously noted, at the transition point between the two stages, a rapid rise in V
LIB is observed while I
LIB does not vary. This is followed by an abrupt decrease of both V
LIB and I
LIB , completing the charging process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BMS behavior during the charging phases
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 4 and 5 show the evolution of GI, P
PV , P
LIB , and SOC on DOYs 124 and 125, respectively. In addition to these parameters, P
PV_est1 , P
lost , E
LIB_ACC , and E
lost_ACC are also depicted, with these terms defined hereafter. P
PV_est1 corresponds to the PV power estimated from the measured GI values and the operation of the PV field in the MPP, as determined and validated in [45]. The term P
lost , defined by (2), is the difference between P
PV_est1 and P
LIB and represents the PV power that could be potentially available during the charge stage. P
LIB is only slightly lower than P
PV (and P
PV_est1 ) during fast charging. The energy introduced (or delivered) into the battery in a sampling interval (E
LIB,k , in Wh) is calculated in (3) considering the LIB power (P
LIB,k ). The energy accumulated in the LIB in each sampling interval, denoted as E
LIB_ACC,k (in Wh), is calculated by (4). Following a similar approach, the accumulated energy loss (E
lost_ACC,k , in Wh) is calculated by (5). At the beginning of each day, both accumulated energies are initialized to zero in both figures, while the SOC representation displays the absolute value of this variable. The value of E
LIB_ACC,k can vary between 0 kWh (indicating a fully discharged LIB or the start of a new charging cycle) and 11.5 kWh, which corresponds to the nominal capacity of the LIB used in the test shown in [44].
(2)

At the end of the fast charge stage, P
LIB falls to very low values, followed by P
PV . This increases the difference between P
LIB and P
PV_est1 and raises losses in the system (E
lost_ACC ) until a stabilization begins at the end of the day when radiation decreases. This is the slow charge stage. When radiation reaches zero, pumping starts, and fast discharge begins.

Throughout the slow charge state, with a SOC of between 98% and 100%, two BMS behaviors were observed in relation to the control of the PV point of operation established for completing the LIB charging. These two BMS behaviors may be due to the various charge controls implemented by the manufacturer in these final stages of charging, which could be classified as multistage constant currents or pulse charging, as indicated in [37]. On DOYs 122 or 124, after achieving a full LIB charge (SOC = 100%), the P
PV stabilized at a minimum value around 40 W, as illustrated in Fig. 4 for DOY 124. For the other days analyzed, such as DOY 125 (Fig. 5), the SOC remained consistently high, fluctuating between 97% and 98% as a result of oscillations in P
PV and P
LIB induced by the BMS. The peak values of these oscillations reached approximately P
PV = 266 W and P
LIB = 241 W, coinciding with the minimum values of the SOC at around 97%. In contrast, the minimum values within the oscillation cycles were about 36 W for P
PV and 1.1 W for P
LIB , with average values of approximately 94 W and 52 W, respectively. These results indicate that the system frequently operates in a slow-charging region near full SOC, leading to PV energy losses due to MPP deviation and contributing to accelerate battery aging in accordance with [31,35]. However, this conclusion is inherently linked to the specific battery chemistry, BMS control strategy, and operating conditions of the hybrid system evaluated in this study, and may not be directly generalizable to other technologies or configurations. Avoiding this slow-charging region—potentially by increasing AC consumption to better match PV availability, for instance by raising the pumped flow rate or adding additional loads—could mitigate these effects in this particular system. Nonetheless, broader validation would require long-term data and tests on comparable hybrid architectures from different manufacturers.

From Figs. 4 and 5, it follows that P
LIB during slow charging remains constant on certain days, such as DOY 124 (Fig. 4, from 15:00 to 21:41), while on other days (such as DOY 125) it fluctuates (Fig. 5, ripple in P
LIB from 15:12 to 19:52). This differentiated behavior is also reflected in the way 100% SOC is reached and in the evolution of E
LIB_ACC , which presents a smooth slope in Fig. 5 that persists until the battery reaches 100% SOC. At this point, the oscillations previously observed in the power signals disappear, indicating the completion of the charging process.

P
PV_est1 and P
PV evolve in parallel with P
LIB until the fast charge ends, demonstrating the correct operation of the MPP tracking algorithm. As expected, P
LIB always remains below P
PV , highlighting the system's charging efficiency. From the end of fast charging, both P
PV and P
LIB drop sharply, which causes a sudden increase in system losses (P
lost ). From that moment onwards, P
lost follows the evolution of P
PV_est1 , which shows the PV power produced by the system. The P
lost value increases sharply because less PV power is needed to complete the LIB charge from the setpoint values established by the BMS. Figs. 6 and 7 , enlarged to show greater detail, depict the region around the moment the fast charge ends and the slow charge starts on DOYs 124 and 125 (as shown in Figs. 4 and 5).

During battery discharge, the standby consumption in the system (P
standby ) must be considered. The P
standby is due to AC loads connected to the EPS output, such as the monitoring system and the hybrid inverter itself. The P
standby remains fairly constant throughout the day and is characterized by stable and very low current values, which can be measured when both P
PV and P
VSD_in are zero. During the entire experimental test, these values ranged between 17.28 W and 32.20 W (23.77 W on average), corresponding to a current between 73.33 mA and 136.67 mA (100.88 mA on average). VSD operation was carried out at constant power, determined by f
VSD . The start of fast discharge is observed with the negative values of P
LIB and I
LIB along with a progressive decrease in V
LIB and SOC. Fig. 8 shows the evolution of the main parameters of the system during both the charge and discharge tests performed on the first day of study (DOY122) with f
VSD at its nominal value (f
VSD = 50 Hz), demanding an active power in the AC output of the hybrid inverter (P
EPS_out ) equal to 2240 W.

Table 3 presents the most representative parameters observed during the LIB discharge process. The variable
t

refers to the time elapsed from the beginning of the discharge – starting with a fully charged battery (SOC close to 100%) – until the SOC reaches its final value, which varies depending on the test day. The apparent average efficiency of the DC/AC converter (

) is defined in (6). P

EPS_out provided by the SOLAX hybrid inverter correspond to values between the active power and the apparent power demanded by the AC loads. Due to this discrepancy in P
EPS_out , the values of the apparent
η

included in Table 3 are slightly higher than the real values and, in some cases, are close to 100%, the theoretical maximum value.

The last two rows of Table 3 present the pumping duration and the corresponding pumped volume delivered during the discharge phase for a SOC change from 98% to 41% (denoted as
t
SOC
98 % → 41 %
and
V
p
98 % → 41 %
, respectively). This SOC range was selected because it is consistently available across all test days, ensuring comparability under all test conditions. A clear inverse relationship is observed between f
VSD and both the pumping time and the volume delivered. At 37 Hz, the system exhibits the longest pumping duration (343 min) and the greatest volume output (555.2 L), whereas at 50 Hz, these values decrease to 137 min and 367.8 L, respectively. This trend indicates that a low f
VSD favors extended discharge periods and greater fluid delivery, which aligns with the findings of [47], where optimal system efficiency was reported at 37 Hz. The greater volume pumped at lower frequencies suggests improved energy conversion and utilization during discharge, reinforcing the operational advantage of low f
VSD settings for maximizing system performance under constant power conditions.

Fig. 9 illustrates the interrelation between SOC and V
LIB during discharge tests at four constant powers, determined by the operating frequency set by the VSD controlling the pump (f
VSD ). It is observed that V
LIB gradually decreases as the discharge progresses (decreasing SOC values), showing less uniformity in the slope (more pronounced steps), the higher the discharge power, but generally following a linear trend of decreasing slope, similar to that described in [52] although under different discharging conditions). V
LIB then depends on the SOC, while the SOC itself is estimated by the SOLAX control algorithm that uses V
LIB among other system parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between ILIB and VLIB during fast charging and discharging stages
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 10 illustrates the relationship between I
LIB and V
LIB on sunny days (DOY 122 and DOY 125) during the fast charge stage (from the start of charge until V
LIB reaches 244 V), with the PV field operating at the MPP. It verifies that as I
LIB increases, V
LIB also increases. On sunny days, the I
LIB progressively increases as GI and P
PV rise, until noon, when the LIB reaches full charge as indicated by the SOC reaching 100% in Fig. 1. This charging behavior reflects the direct influence of solar irradiance on the charging current, culminating in full battery saturation around midday. Based on the plot in Fig. 10 and the generic battery model from [31,53,54], the fitting function shown in (7) is derived, where

A more comprehensive resistive equivalent model, which incorporates three resistors and their relationship to the electrochemical processes of the battery, is detailed in [29]. In contrast, [55] presents a simplified model that includes an additional RC network to account for the dynamic response of the LIB. During battery charging, the voltage drop at

During the discharge stage, while the VSD is in operation at constant power, the value of I

The fitting function shown in (8) represents the characteristic equation of the battery during the discharge stage, analogous to the fitting function presented in (7), which was deduced for the charge stage. This observation suggests that it may be preferable to charge the battery more slowly over a longer period of time.

In the PVWPS+LIB(HV) facilities described in [56], a slower and more efficient battery charging can be achieved by controlling the connection of AC loads to the output of the hybrid inverter. In these systems, part of the PV power is used to charge the battery while the remainder is converted into AC power. The AC consumption can be managed either by adjusting the operating conditions of the VSD (by means of f
VSD ) or by varying the number and characteristics of other connected loads. These loads may include power banks charged in the AC output, enabling mobile electrification for communities in remote areas and humanitarian aid scenarios, as illustrated in Fig. 12 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion about efficiencies and performances in the system
ce:section.ce:para:
In the following subsections, the efficiency of the LIB (
η LIB
) and its performance ratio (PR
LIB ) are assessed. The analysis begins with an evaluation of the charging and discharging efficiencies of the LIB. The useful capacity—defined as the total energy that can be stored or delivered during these processes—is then estimated using various SOC windows through a recursive approach, which can be categorized as a segment-to-point approach according to [55]. Finally, PR
LIB is calculated and compared for both fast and slow charging phases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficiency of the charging and discharging processes
ce:section.ce:para:
Efficiency during the LIB charge (
η
LIB _ CH
), as depicted in Fig. 13 , is calculated with (9). Both powers in the hybrid inverter terminals are measured and expressed as a percentage. The behavior is similar to the typical PV inverter efficiency described in [50,57]. As charge and discharge do not overlap in the tests performed,
η
LIB _ CH
does not depend on the rate at which the LIB was previously discharged, although on DOY 123 (charge following a discharge at 37 Hz, lower discharge power), some points outside the general pattern are observed, which were not considered relevant in this study.
(9)

P
PV , k

The average values of
η
LIB _ CH
(
η
LIB _ CH _ AV
) at different P
LIB ranges (given as mean ± standard deviation, m ± SD), are summarized in Table 4 . To better detect the pronounced variability of
η
LIB _ CH
at low power levels, additional data points were included for P
LIB < 500 W. The results clearly indicate that charge efficiency improves significantly with increasing P
LIB . In particular,
η
LIB _ CH _ AV
exceeds 0.94 for P
LIB values above 500 W and stabilizes around 0.96 for P
LIB ≥ 1000 W. This highlights that high power charging is significantly more efficient. However, in PVWPS, where the available power fluctuates throughout the day, the battery is frequently charged under low-power conditions. Therefore, an accurate modeling of the efficiency in this region is essential for realistically assessing the performance of PVWPS+LIB(HV) systems.

The apparent LIB discharging efficiency (
η
LIB _ DCH
), illustrated in Fig. 14 , is calculated using (10), where both power values are measured at the terminals of the hybrid inverter and expressed as a percentage. The absolute value of P
LIB is used to eliminate the negative sign associated with discharge power. The maximum allowed discharge power in the tested hybrid inverter model is 3.5 kW. Fig. 14 presents
η
LIB _ DCH
as a function of P
LIB for the four f
VSD selected to set the constant power during discharge: 50 Hz, 45 Hz, 40 Hz, and 37 Hz. The results show a clear trend: discharge efficiency increases as the output power decreases (higher efficiency for low f
VSD values). The highest efficiencies – close to 100% – are observed at 37 Hz, the lowest VSD operating frequency noted in the tests, and which corresponds to the lowest power demand that is required for pumping water.
(10)

P LIB

The average values of the apparent
η
LIB _ DCH
are aligned with the DC/AC conversion efficiencies (
η
DC / AC _ AV
) presented in Table 3. However, some instantaneous values near 100% are likely due to the previously discussed discrepancy of P
EPS_out , which becomes more evident in this figure. Despite this miscalculation, as seen in Fig. 14, the LIB discharging efficiency improves at lower EPS output power levels, where the VSD is the primary load, with a 1.8% increase under these conditions. The greater efficiency for the hybrid inverter with the VSD operating at f
VSD equal to 37 Hz (low power demand) agrees with the results presented in [47,57]. These findings underscore the importance of accounting for load-dependent inverter behavior in system-level efficiency modeling and identifying the operating point that maximizes efficiency, commonly referred to as the solar best efficiency point (SBEP) [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of the battery capacity and round-trip performance ratio
ce:section.ce:para:
The total amount of energy that could be stored or delivered by the LIB was evaluated by analyzing the energy entering the battery when charging and the energy supplied during discharging, using several SOC windows (W
SOC ). The values of W
SOC used in the study are the following: 1%, 10%, 20%, 25%, and 40%. This assessment was based on experimental data collected by the monitoring system from the hybrid inverter during the test days, as shown in Fig. 15 with a t
k = 1 min, corresponding to a zoomed view of the charge process on DOY 122. The small difference between P
PV and P
LIB corresponds to the energy losses in the DC/DC conversion. E
LIB,k , calculated with (3), varies between 25 Wh and 35 Wh in Fig. 15 following the increase in the GI during the central hours of this sunny day. E
LIB_ACC,k , calculated with (4), follows the evolution of PV power, increasing linearly as expected on a cloudless day. The continuous line at the lowest plot of Fig. 15 represents the SOC values recorded by the monitoring system, and the red markers indicate the instances when the SOC value changed by 1% (the minimum W
SOC value used in the calculations). The subscript j in the term SOC
j refers to the first red marker of each W SOC used in the calculation procedure described in the paragraphs below.

The segment-to-point calculation process starts with the initial SOC (or SOC
i ). In this case, the subscript i designates both the SOC value at the beginning of the charge tests (which varied between 22% and 39% depending on the test conditions), and also the SOC value at the start of the discharge tests (which ranged from 100% to 98%), as detailed in Table 1. The recursive procedure for estimating battery capacity is described next with reference to the graphical signals plotted at the bottom of Fig. 15. Denoting (j + W
SOC ) as the instant time at which the SOC has changed by W SOC from j, the energy that has entered into the battery producing the SOC variation defined by W
SOC is calculated with (11) as the difference between the accumulated energy in (j + W
SOC ), and the accumulated energy in (j).

A comparative analysis of the estimated total battery capacity considering W
SOC = 1% during charge and discharge (Figs. 17 and 18 , respectively) reveals consistent differences between the two processes. The values of C
LIB_est,⁎ obtained for each j, deduced from charge data, are notably higher and show greater dispersion than those from discharge data. This discrepancy highlights the impact of internal losses and conversion inefficiencies, particularly under real operating conditions. A high degree of variability is observed in both cases, with no consistent trend across the SOC range. However, during charging, a slight reduction in energy per 1% SOC is observed at higher SOC levels (above ~85%), suggesting a decrease in charge acceptance as the battery approaches full capacity. Despite this, the dispersion of values indicates that using W
SOC = 1% to estimate total battery capacity introduces significant uncertainty and is not recommended for precise battery capacity estimation, modeling, or system sizing. Due to the poor stability observed in the capacity estimates obtained with W
SOC = 1%, the use of low W
SOC values is not recommended for accurate battery-capacity estimation, modeling, or system sizing. As shown in Table 5 , the capacity estimated with W
SOC = 1% exhibits a markedly higher SD (3551.4 Wh, equivalent to 30% of the nominal capacity of the LIB) compared with the values obtained using the other W
SOC evaluated in this study, all of which remain below 10%. This large variability indicates that WSOC as low as 1% provide unreliable and non-representative capacity assessments.

Figs. 19 and 20 depict the values of C
LIB_est,⁎ obtained with W
SOC = 25% during charge and discharge tests, respectively. These values represent the total energy, and as expected, a more stable estimate of battery capacity is obtained compared to smaller W
SOC values. It can be verified that as W
SOC increases, the dispersion of values decreases, and clearer trends emerge in the evolution of both charge and discharge. In the case of charge (Fig. 19), the estimated energy values converge around 11.5 kWh, which closely matches the nominal capacity of the battery. The influence of solar irradiance during the charge is reflected in the distribution of data points in Fig. 19. Specifically, the values corresponding to the sunniest day (DOY 122, PSH = 6.91) are concentrated in the lower region of the graph and exhibit a more uniform trend around the average value indicated by the manufacturer, which shows that the calculation is more accurate when the charge is performed under more stable conditions (with low variations in GI). In contrast, data from cloudiest days (DOYs 124, 126, and 127, with PSH values of 5.39, 6.08, and 4.94, respectively) are located in the upper region of the graph, suggesting that the LIB has greater energy storage capacity and exhibits a more pronounced downward trend, particularly towards the end of the charging process (i.e., for SOC > 60%) and this is likely due to reduced charge acceptance at higher SOC levels.

While irradiance conditions (which, in turn, affect P
PV and P
LIB_CH ) impact the amount of effective energy stored during charging, they do not influence the energy delivered during discharge (Fig. 20), as the battery is discharged in the absence of PV generation. The use of a constant f
VSD during discharge contributes to the greater uniformity observed in C
LIB_est,⁎ on the different days. In general, the results for discharging are more consistent than those for charging for all analyzed W
SOC values, except for the smallest interval (W
SOC = 1%). Additionally, the moment at which the considered calculation window starts plays a notable role: higher initial SOC values tend to yield higher energy estimates that are closer to the nominal capacity, suggesting that both the size of W
SOC used for estimating available energy, and the specific SOC
j value, are critical factors for accurately characterizing the battery.

Table 5 summarizes the total energy that the LIB can store and release with various W
SOC intervals expressed as
C
LI
B est
AV
in Wh (mean ± SD), along with the corresponding performance ratio (PR
LIB ) defined in (1).

The variation in the estimated LIB capacity, calculated with different W
SOC values, is relatively small when considering the energy accumulated during charging and when addressing the energy potentially recoverable during discharging. However, the data dispersion associated with these averages is notably higher when using narrow SOC intervals (e.g., W
SOC = 1%) and decreases rapidly as the interval width increases. The C
LIB_est AV values during charging with W
SOC above 10% are quite homogeneous and range between 11,401.1 Wh (W
SOC = 10%) and 11,491.3 Wh (W
SOC = 25%). Concerning discharge, C
LIB_est AV shows less uniformity, varying between 9462.3 Wh (W
SOC = 40%) and 9775.2 Wh (W
SOC = 10%). In summary, the charge values obtained are close to the nominal capacity (11,500 Wh), while the discharge behavior corresponds to a full depth of discharge (100%), assuming a usable capacity of 10,350 Wh [44].

The dispersion of the data with W
SOC = 1% causes some PR
LIB values to be out of range (0 < PR
LIB < 1). Days characterized by higher cloud cover (DOY 127 and DOY 124) exhibit slightly lower PR
LIB values across all W
SOC ranges. Although PR
LIB remains relatively uniform with W
SOC ≥ 10%, a decreasing trend is observed as W
SOC increases, with an overall mean value of 0.836 (compared to the manufacturer's reported round-trip efficiency of 95% in [44]). Table 6 complements Table 5 by providing values for
C
LI
B
_ est
AV
for larger and fixed W
SOC . In Table 6, the column labeled ∆SOC
day (%) represents the total SOC variation observed during the charge and discharge cycles on each test day. The corresponding C
LIB_day (Wh) column provides the estimated LIB capacity obtained with this daily SOC variation, calculated as the difference of the accumulated energies between the extremes of the process, reflecting the total amount of energy stored and delivered by the battery under real operating conditions. The columns labeled W
SOC indicate the fixed SOC window used to estimate the total energy that can be stored and delivered by the battery on each day during the charging and subsequent discharging phases. For example, on DOY 122, the C
LIB_est when considering the 40➔80% SOC range is approximately 10,934.7 Wh, while for the 40➔98% range, it drops to 8626.0 Wh. This reduction can be attributed to the BMS having difficulty controlling the final LIB charging process and estimating the actual SOC variation corresponding to the LIB operating voltage and current under those circumstances, as evidenced by the consistently low values (< 1 kWh) in the 95➔98% range. These findings support the recommendation to limit the maximum SOC considered in the operating battery capacity calculations, consistent with practices applied to lead-acid batteries, where SOC > 90% is typically considered as full charge [56], or in the SOC range limits imposed for a vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) in [23].

The results depicted in Tables 5 and 6 also reveal that LIB capacity variability during charging is higher than during discharging, primarily due to fluctuations in solar irradiance that result in variations in charge power. It seems that on cloudier days (DOYs 127, 124, and 126) the total amount of
C

shows values higher than the nominal LIB capacity, while days with higher irradiance levels (such as DOY 122) lead to lower
C

values, showing lower variability in the storage process according to the results depicted in Fig. 19 for W
SOC = 25% during charge. For example, under high irradiance conditions on DOY 122,

using a W SOC = 20% was 10,918.7 ± 417.7 Wh, whereas on the cloudier DOY 127, it increased to 11,885.9 ± 1309.6 Wh. This confirms again that stable and intense solar input leads to more consistent charging behavior, while fluctuating irradiance on cloudy days introduces greater variability in energy accumulation, which is reflected in the value obtained for

. In contrast, as already mentioned, when discharge conditions are kept constant (fixed f

It is important to highlight that the estimate of the energy that can be stored or supplied by the battery depends on the W
SOC used. A narrower W
SOC would tend to underestimate stored energy during charging and overestimate it during discharging, leading to overestimated PR
LIB values that may not accurately reflect system performance. Therefore, it is advisable to use larger W
SOC values for PR
LIB estimation, while avoiding those that include SOC values typically associated with full charge conditions (> 95%) or that extend beyond 80%, where charge acceptance becomes less effective.

As previously discussed, SOC values between 80% and 95% often exhibit rapid increases that do not correspond to proportional energy input, resulting in inconsistent estimates. This issue is even more pronounced in the 95–98% range. To ensure realistic estimates of total
C
LI
B est
, it is recommended to use values of W
SOC of at least 10% and preferably higher (such as 20%, 25%, 40%, and 40–95%), which provide stable and representative values under real operating conditions. On average, these intervals result in a charging capacity of 11,402.5 Wh (close to the nominal 11,500 Wh), a useful capacity of 9587.7 Wh during discharge, and a performance ratio (PR
LIB ) of 0.842 (compared to the manufacturer's reported round-trip efficiency of 95%). Values for full cycle efficiency described in [25] for several manufacturers of residential BESS connected to the grid are in the range of 88.5% to 90% for inverters of 5 kW and LIB capacities in the range of 5 kWh to 15 kWh. However, the round-trip efficiency range in other studies, such as [58], with various LIB technologies, ranges from 70% to 95%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LIB performance during the fast and slow charging phases
ce:section.ce:para:
Table 7 presents a detailed summary of the main parameters that characterize the system's behavior during the fast-charging phase (until the SOC reaches approximately 87–95%) and the subsequent slow charging phase. For each phase, the following terms are included: charging duration; global tilted irradiation (GTI); PV energy generated (E
PV ); energy stored in the battery (E
LIB ); energy consumed by the power conditioning unit (E
PCU ); estimated PV energy under ideal MPPT conditions (E
PV_est ); and PV energy lost (or not generated) during each period (E
lost , defined in (14)). The values of E
PV_est were calculated following the methodology described in [45]. Additionally, the performance ratio of the charging process (PR
CH ) is calculated using (15), while an estimated PR for the slow charging phase (PR
S_CH_est ), which considers the total PV energy potentially generated under the given GI conditions, is defined in (16). These metrics are reported in Table 7 for each test day, along with the corresponding daily PSH and the overall averages.

Regarding performance ratio metrics, during fast charging, PR
CH values are consistently high, ranging between 0.954 and 0.961 (with an average of 0.957). These values are similar to those provided by the manufacturer [44]. This reflects a highly efficient energy transfer from the PV array to the battery. In contrast, the slow charging phase exhibits notably different behavior. The estimated PR
S_CH_est ranges from 0.003 to 0.081. These extremely low values indicate that only a very small fraction of the available PV energy is actually stored in the battery during this phase. These results reinforce the conclusion that the system is highly inefficient during slow charging. Therefore, SOC intervals that include periods of slow charging should not be used to estimate the charge and discharge capacity of a LIB in real operating conditions. As demonstrated above, doing so leads to results that significantly deviate from the observed behavior of the system. The energy stored during slow charge phases is drastically lower due to curtailment and limited energy utilization, which distorts the estimation of the battery's usable capacity and can result in misleading conclusions about system efficiency and performance ratios.

In recent years, the use of lithium-ion batteries has gained popularity in PV systems, both in grid-connected and stand-alone applications. While the literature on this topic is extensive, experimental studies involving high-capacity high-voltage LIBs remain essential to refine equivalent models and define operating parameters that enhance the accuracy of performance estimates in more complex or higher-power applications. Key aspects include charge and discharge efficiencies, DC/DC and DC/AC conversion efficiencies, system performance, usable capacity, and the integration of LIBs in PV systems.

This study presents experimental results obtained using a multipurpose PV water pumping system equipped with a high-voltage LIB with a capacity of 11.5 kWh and operating at 230.4 V (nominal values). Independent tests were conducted for charging and discharging to better characterize each process. Charging was performed using exclusively PV energy, with no AC consumption except for the monitoring system and the hybrid inverter's own consumption. Discharging was carried out at constant power using the VSD of the water pumping system. The SOC at the beginning of charging ranged from 22% to 39%, and the final SOC after discharging ranged from 22% to 41%.

Two distinct phases were identified during the charging process. In the first phase, the PV field operated at the maximum power point (MPP), and the battery charged efficiently up to SOC values of between 87% to 95%. In the second phase, as the battery approached full charge, the PV system deviated from MPP due to curtailment imposed by the BMS, resulting in significant energy losses. This transition was marked by a sharp voltage increase and a drop in PV power, which no longer followed irradiance levels.

The comparative study of V
LIB with respect to I
LIB enabled the extraction of parameters for a generic battery model. Notably, the internal resistance and the open circuit counter-electromotive/electromotive force of the battery differed between charging (1.13 Ω, 233.9 V) and discharging (0.41 Ω, 236.5 V), highlighting the need for distinct modeling approaches for each process.

The efficiency of the DC/DC converters managing MPPT and LIB charging followed a typical curve. For power levels above 500 W, efficiency exceeded 94%, peaking at 95% near 2 kW. Efficiency declined rapidly in the range below 500 W, and was characterized in detail to improve system modeling under low GI conditions. During discharging, the highest efficiency was obtained when the VSD operated at 37 Hz (lowest power consumption). However, inconsistencies in the hybrid inverter's monitoring data, which sometimes report efficiencies above 100%, suggest inaccuracies in the reported active power. A 1.8% efficiency improvement was observed when operating at a range of 1 kW compared to the maximum power of 2.3 kW. This supports the recommendation to operate the VSD at low power levels, which also aligns with the optimal efficiency point (SBEP) of the water pumping system [47]. Although the pumped flow rate was reduced, longer pumping durations could compensate for this.

An estimation of the operating useful capacity during charging and discharging was calculated using several SOC windows (W
SOC equal to 1%, 10%, 20%, 25%, and 40%) through a recursive calculation. The results with a W
SOC = 1% exhibited high variability and are not recommended for capacity estimation or performance analysis. Using a larger W
SOC yielded more stable results closely matching the manufacturer's nominal capacity. The variability of irradiance on cloudy days affected the charging process, leading to overestimated LIB capacity. In contrast, more stable capacity values, closer to the nominal ones, were obtained on sunny days when charging was more uniform. The use of specific W
SOC to estimate battery capacity concluded that the SOC range of 40% to 80% provided more stable numerical values close to the nominal capacity, while the final charging stages (SOC > 80%) should be avoided due to significant energy losses and underestimation of capacity. The numerical values obtained with the proposed algorithm enable their comparison with the manufacturer's nominal capacity and will be used in future studies to determine some of the factors defined in [59] related to the aging of a LIB throughout its useful life.

The analysis of performance ratios during charging further confirmed these findings. When the BMS accepted all PV power, the system operated efficiently. However, in the final charging stages, the PV energy generated was significantly reduced, as shown in Table 7, with an average of 0.5 kWh consumed compared to the 4.4 kWh that was curtailed, representing more than 50% of the PV energy generated during fast charging (8.1 kWh on average). The performance ratio dropped from 0.957 during fast charging to just 0.048 during slow charging, with energy losses increasing tenfold. This highlights the need for diversion loads that can be activated when SOC exceeds 80%, to make better use of surplus PV energy. The increase in AC consumption can be achieved by operating the VSD at frequencies higher than that of the SBEP (f
VSD > 37 Hz), which enables the power to be adjusted between 990 W and 2240 W, as indicated in Table 1. If water requirements have already been met and higher flow rates are not needed, AC consumption can also be increased by charging the power banks used for basic electrification in these communities. In summary, the results of this study provide valuable insights for the design, modeling, and operation of PV systems with high-voltage LIB storage. The findings can be integrated into AI-based diagnostic tools to monitor battery capacity degradation, assess performance over time, and support decision-making regarding battery maintenance or replacement. Ultimately, this helps ensure that the system continues to meet user energy demands efficiently throughout the battery's operational life.
